sections.content:
Technology is where I tend to feel most at ease, which is also where it's easiest to stop questioning yourself. I feel very at ease at high level coding and electrical components. Examples of this are my Obsidian plugin and tactone.

The Obsidian plugin started from a gap I noticed in a tool I use every day: no plugin existed for viewing 3D models inside notes. So I built one. What began as a proof-of-concept eventually became a proper TypeScript plugin with configurable scene parameters, and it now has over 2400 installations. You write code differently when real users file bug reports and expect a response. Version management, community communication, gradual feature rollout, none of that comes up in coursework. It was technically driven: I noticed a problem I had, built a solution, others had the same problem. That worked. But it's a different process than designing for people whose needs you've actually researched, a distinction I became more aware of through the master.

Tactone pushed into different territory, pneumatic actuation, LDR sensor processing, audio modulation, custom silicone pads, all in one coherent system. I tried to make it a reusable platform rather than a one-off: the guide explains how to swap components, upload audio, recalibrate for different environments. Writing that forced a different kind of understanding than just building it. The user study surfaced issues I hadn't anticipated, and the setup sequence mattered more than I expected, both pointing to something I keep running into: in physical-digital systems, the seams matter as much as the components.

A thread I want to keep pulling: the gap between technically-driven and human-centered design is something my identity explicitly names as a tension. The direction I want to move is continuing to let both inform each other.
